title: Brioche suisse
subtitle: Swiss-style brioche filled with vanilla pastry cream and chocolate chips
excerpt: Brioche suisse are individual pastries from Swiss and French pâtisseries: a thin sheet of brioche dough spread with vanilla pastry cream and scattered with chocolate chips, then folded over itself, cut into rectangles, and proved until pillowy. Baked, they peel apart in layers, giving alternating panels of soft brioche, creamy filling, and melted chocolate in each bite. They are a patisserie pastry that can be made at home with one long session on the day before.
sourceNotes: Brioche suisse belongs to the viennoiserie tradition of Swiss and French pâtisseries and is associated with French-speaking Switzerland and the Lyon-Geneva corridor. It appears in French patisserie manuals of the twentieth century as a variant on brioche Nanterre adapted to include a pastry cream (crème pâtissière) filling. The combination of pastry cream and chocolate chips became the standard version in Swiss bakeries from the 1970s.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat at 160 degrees C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The brioche dough benefits from a cold overnight fermentation in the fridge after shaping. Fill, cut, prove, and bake the next morning.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thick, stiff pastry cream made by cooking milk, egg yolks, sugar, and starch — usually cornflour — together until the mixture boils and sets. Unlike a pouring custard, crème pâtissière holds its shape when cold and is used to fill pastries, tarts, and choux. The starch protects the egg yolks from curdling, so the mixture can be taken to a full boil without scrambling. It must be cooled quickly, with cling film pressed directly onto the surface to prevent a skin forming.

content.text: The brioche dough is cold when you roll it; chilling firms the butter and makes the dough easier to handle than soft brioche. The

must also be cold and stiff before spreading; warm pastry cream runs. The fold-and-cut method is the same as for pain suisse; a large sheet folded over itself, then sliced into individual rectangles so each piece has filling on one side and plain brioche on the other, sealed when they puff together during proving.

Mix 350 g flour, 7 g yeast, 35 g sugar, and 5 g salt. Add 100 ml warm milk and 3 beaten eggs. Knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Add 120 g softened butter in small pieces, kneading well after each addition, until the dough is smooth, glossy, and pulls away from the bowl. Cover and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes until nearly doubled. Knock back, shape into a flat block, wrap, and refrigerate for at least 2 hours or overnight.

Heat 300 ml milk to just below simmering. Whisk 3 egg yolks, 70 g sugar, 30 g cornflour, and 1 tsp vanilla together. Pour the hot milk over in a thin stream, whisking. Return to medium heat and stir continuously until the mixture thickens, boils, and bubbles for 1 minute. Remove from the heat and stir in 20 g butter. Press cling film directly onto the surface and refrigerate until cold and firm.

On a lightly floured surface, roll the cold brioche dough to a rectangle about 35 × 25 cm and 4 mm thick. Spread the cold crème pâtissière over one half of the rectangle lengthways, leaving a 1 cm border at the edges. Scatter 100 g chocolate chips over the cream. Fold the plain half of the dough over the filling and press gently to seal. Refrigerate for 15 minutes to firm. Cut into 10 rectangles, each about 7 cm wide. Place well-spaced on lined baking trays. Cover and prove at room temperature for 45 to 50 minutes until puffed. Heat the oven to 175 degrees C fan. Brush with beaten egg. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until deep golden. Cool on a rack.

items.solution: The dough was not sealed tightly enough at the edges, or the pastry cream was too warm and runny. Chill the assembled sheet for 15 minutes before cutting, and press the cut edges firmly together with your fingertips.
The prove was too short. Brioche suisse should look noticeably puffed before going into the oven — if they look the same size as when cut, give them another 15 to 20 minutes.

items.problem: Pastry cream has a lumpy texture after chilling
items.solution: The mixture was not stirred continuously during cooking, or the heat was too low and the starch did not cook out fully. Bring the mixture to a full boil — one minute at a steady boil ensures the cornflour is properly cooked and the cream will be smooth when cold.
